chapter: Chapter 2: The Social War and the Italian Question (91-88 BCE)

questions.question: Which of the following were benefits of Roman citizenship that the allies lacked? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: The right to vote in Roman assemblies
The right to appeal against arbitrary punishment
The right to own property in their home communities
The right to marry other Roman citizens legally
The right to have disputes heard in Roman courts

questions.question: Which factors contributed to Rome's refusal to extend citizenship to the allies? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: Belief that citizenship required physical participation in Rome
Fear of disrupting existing political alliances and voting blocs
Economic interests in maintaining preferential citizen access to land
Religious prohibition against sharing sacred rites with foreigners
Inertia from centuries of a successful allied system

questions.question: Which groups opposed Livius Drusus's reform program? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: The equestrian order who controlled jury courts
Senators who distrusted his methods
Italian allies who wanted independence, not citizenship
Roman common people hostile to sharing their status
Military commanders who feared losing their veterans

questions.question: Which Italian peoples formed the core of the rebellion during the Social War? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: The Marsi
The Etruscans
The Samnites
The Umbrians
The Lucanians
The Greek cities of the south

questions.question: Which laws extended citizenship or related rights during and after the Social War? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: Lex Julia de Civitate
Lex Plautia Papiria
Lex Pompeia
Lex Hortensia
Lex Sempronia

questions.question: What institutions did the rebel confederation Italia create that mirrored Rome? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: A senate of 500 members
Annually elected consuls
Praetors and other magistrates
A permanent standing army
Its own coinage

questions.question: Which Roman commanders built their military reputations during the Social War? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: Lucius Cornelius Sulla
Gnaeus Pompeius Strabo
Gaius Julius Caesar (the dictator)
Gaius Marius
Marcus Licinius Crassus

questions.question: What were consequences of the Social War's citizenship grants? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: The number of Roman citizens roughly doubled
New citizens were registered fairly across all voting tribes
Italian aristocrats began entering Roman political life
Traditional patronage networks were disrupted
The divide between citizens and allies eventually faded

questions.question: Why did the Social War make future civil wars more likely? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: It confirmed that professional soldiers followed their generals loyally
It created new citizens who felt cheated by their political marginalization
It elevated military strongmen with loyal veterans and political ambitions
It permanently ended the authority of the Roman Senate
It left the tribal registration question unresolved and contentious

questions.question: Which arguments did reformers use to advocate for extending citizenship to the allies? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: The allies had earned citizenship through military sacrifice
Extending citizenship would solve the veteran land distribution problem
Discontented allies posed a security risk to Rome
Foreign powers were offering citizenship to lure allies away
The manpower crisis would worsen if Italy remained divided
